Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings, but with my extracurriculars and limited resources at home, it was hard to stick to anything consistent. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend time practicing problems or working through things physically rather than just reading. I aimed for around 15 hours a week, but the quality of that time varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd have these bursts of activity when something was due, then things would get quieter. Regular times were hard to maintain because I have other commitments outside of class, and sometimes my internet access at home isn't the most reliable for streaming videos or staying logged in for long periods. I tried to create some consistency, but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed, especially since my attendance in actual classes wasn't great. I'd also go in when I needed to download materials to study offline later. It wasn't really a habit I looked forward to—more like something I did because I had to.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in every couple of days, especially early in the semester when I was trying to stay on top of things. But honestly, it never felt natural to me. The CBT sessions I had helped me recognize some patterns in my avoidance, which made me more aware of when I was putting things off. Still, the LMS never became something I genuinely integrated into my daily life.
Q5: The downloadable materials and practice problems were the most useful for me because I learn best by doing things with my hands. I'd print stuff out or work through problems on paper rather than just watching videos. The discussion posts were surprisingly helpful too—seeing how other students approached things gave me different perspectives. Videos were okay, but I preferred having something I could physically interact with.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched parts of them. With my internet situation, streaming wasn't always smooth, and I found it hard to stay focused just watching someone talk. Some of the supplementary readings I ignored because they felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. If something didn't seem directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd usually push it aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with. Anything that let me practice and apply concepts was valuable to me. I also appreciated things that didn't require me to be online the whole time—if I could download it and work through it at my own pace, that made a big difference given my situation.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my stress levels and everything else going on, I had to prioritize what was required just to keep my head above water. Required activities had this weight to them that forced me to engage even when I didn't feel like it. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got pushed to 'maybe later'—and later rarely came.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print out problem sets, and work through things with pen and paper. I used my textbook a lot and would sometimes look up YouTube videos on my phone when the LMS videos weren't working well for me. I also studied with classmates in person when I could, which helped since I learn better through doing and discussing.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems I'd printed out, or I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes from the materials I'd downloaded. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also have study sessions with peers where we'd work through concepts together physically—drawing diagrams, solving problems on whiteboards. That was some of my most productive learning, but the LMS had no idea it was happening.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture's slides or a reading once, and then that PDF would live on my laptop or get printed out. I'd return to those materials multiple times over weeks, but the system only saw that one download. My exam scores reflect that I was actually learning the material, even though my online engagement looked minimal. It was just more practical for me to work offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was really rough. My stress was through the roof, and I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to focus. My motivation just tanked, and logging into the LMS felt like this huge effort. The CBT sessions helped me recognize I was catastrophizing about falling behind, but even with that awareness, actually engaging felt like pushing through mud. My attendance dropped and I just went into survival mode.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS became the first thing I'd cut corners on. I'd do the bare minimum—check what was due, maybe skim something quickly. My limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I wanted to, and competing responsibilities like my extracurriculars sometimes took priority because they were happening in real-time. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it should have been.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything, or I'd start a video and zone out completely. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things or just too tired to process information. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I told myself at least I was trying.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger something felt, the more I'd avoid starting it. The CBT sessions helped me see this pattern, but knowing about it didn't always stop it. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared of not doing well enough. It's something I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd have these quiet periods where I barely touched the LMS, then a deadline would approach and I'd suddenly be very active—downloading everything, posting in discussions, submitting work. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned. The structure of having clear due dates actually helped me because without them, I might have drifted even more.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on my exam scores, it gave me a boost. It reminded me that I actually understood the material even when I felt like I was barely keeping up. But feedback on assignments was sometimes discouraging because my assignment scores weren't as strong as my exams. I'd engage more after good feedback and kind of retreat after criticism, which I know isn't ideal.
Q18: The course structure with its deadlines and clear requirements helped keep me on track, even if minimally. Having six online courses meant I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. The structure gave me something to work toward, but it also felt rigid sometimes when life got complicated. I appreciated knowing what was expected, even if I didn't always meet those expectations fully.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement way easier for me given my learning style and internet situation. What made it harder was when things required constant online presence or real-time participation—my schedule and resources didn't always allow for that. The discussion requirements were actually good because they forced me to engage with peers, which I found helpful. Video-heavy content was harder for me to stick with.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying but struggling to produce work that meets the standard. They might be researching, reading, watching videos—doing all the 'engagement' activities—but then hit a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. It could be anxiety, confusion about requirements, or just running out of time. High activity doesn't always translate to output, especially when someone's overwhelmed.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity looks pretty low, but I got a distinction because I was learning effectively offline. I'd download what I needed, study on my own terms, and show up prepared for exams. The system couldn't see my printed notes, my study sessions with friends, or the hours I spent working through problems by hand. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they're technically on a page. But they miss so much. They can't tell if someone's actually focused or just has a tab open. They can't see offline studying, group work, or the mental effort someone's putting in. They definitely can't capture whether someone's stressed, anxious, or struggling with things outside of school that affect their engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, asking questions, trying to connect ideas. It doesn't have to look like constant LMS activity—some of my most engaged moments were sitting with my notes at home, working through problems until they clicked. Engagement is internal as much as it is external.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my stress levels or how hard I was working to manage my anxiety about the course. It wouldn't capture the offline hours I spent studying or the peer support that helped me succeed. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation and high stress, I pushed through and actually learned the material well enough to earn a distinction. The numbers tell one story, but my actual experience was much more complicated than what any log could capture.
